title: Governance Fundamentals — Why Security Needs Structure

sections.title: Why Governance Matters
sections.content:
Security governance is the framework that ensures security decisions align with business objectives. Without governance, security becomes reactive — patching holes after breaches instead of preventing them.

**Governance answers:**
- WHO is responsible for security decisions?
- WHAT level of risk is acceptable?
- HOW are security policies enforced?
- WHEN are security reviews conducted?

**The business case:** Organizations with mature governance frameworks experience 53% fewer breaches and recover 40% faster when incidents occur. Governance isn't bureaucracy — it's the difference between organized defense and chaos.

sections.title: Understanding Security Frameworks
sections.content:
Frameworks provide structured approaches to security. They're not checklists — they're thinking tools:

**NIST CSF 2.0** — Organize security into: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover. Best for: organizations starting their security program.

**ISO 27001** — International standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS). Requires certification audit. Best for: organizations needing formal certification.

**CIS Controls v8** — Prioritized, actionable security controls. Implementation Groups (IG1-IG3) scale by maturity. Best for: practical implementation guidance.

**NIST 800-53** — Comprehensive control catalog (1000+ controls). Used by US federal agencies. Best for: detailed control selection.

**When to use which:** Start with NIST CSF for strategy, CIS Controls for quick wins, ISO 27001 for certification, and NIST 800-53 for comprehensive coverage.

sections.title: Security Roles and Responsibilities
sections.content:
Clear ownership prevents the 'someone else's problem' syndrome:

**Board/Executive Level:**
- Sets risk appetite and security strategy
- Approves security budget
- Receives regular security reports

**CISO/Security Director:**
- Translates business risk into security programs
- Manages security team and vendors
- Reports to board on security posture

**Security Team:**
- Implements controls and monitors threats
- Responds to incidents
- Conducts assessments and audits

**All Employees:**
- Follow security policies
- Report suspicious activity
- Complete security training

**Data Owners vs Data Custodians:**
- Owner: Business unit that creates/uses the data (decides classification, access)
- Custodian: IT team that stores/protects the data (implements technical controls)

This separation ensures business context drives security decisions while IT implements them.
